content.text: Technical Debt Register for Claude Teams

Technical debt in Claude integrations is broader than the code debt that software engineering teams typically track. In addition to the standard categories — unclean code, missing tests, outdated dependencies — Claude integrations accumulate a distinct category of prompt debt: system prompts that have been patched incrementally over time until they contain contradictions, dead instructions, and workarounds for model behaviour that has since changed. A technical debt register that does not include prompt debt gives an incomplete picture of the maintenance burden on the team.

attrs.alt: Technical debt register: five debt categories (Prompt, Integration, Test, Model Management, Ops) × three impact tiers (High, Medium, Low)

content.text: The matrix above organises Claude-specific debt categories by impact tier. High-impact debt should be addressed within the current sprint because it is either directly causing user-facing quality failures or blocking quality gates that the team relies on to deploy safely. Medium-impact debt accumulates interest: a missing retry handler is manageable when the API error rate is 0.1% but becomes an incident when it spikes. Low-impact debt should be registered to prevent silent accumulation — teams that have no register for low-impact debt routinely discover it has become high-impact by the time it is noticed.
Prompt Debt: The Claude-Specific Category

Prompt debt is the accumulated cost of incremental system prompt changes that have not been refactored as a unit. A system prompt that was written cleanly at launch and has been patched twelve times over six months often contains instructions that are redundant (the same constraint stated twice in different sections), contradictory (instructions added at different times that conflict with each other), and stale (instructions added to work around a model behaviour that was fixed in a subsequent model version). The cost of prompt debt is poor and unpredictable model behaviour, because the model is attempting to follow a set of instructions that cannot all be simultaneously satisfied.

The refactor_urgency method provides a concrete indicator of when a prompt refactor should be scheduled. Ten patches is not an arbitrary number — it is the point at which a system prompt typically has enough accumulated complexity that the original structure is no longer legible and a new engineer cannot reliably predict how a given instruction will interact with the others. At this point, the correct action is a structured refactor: consolidate the prompt from scratch against the current model version using the current set of requirements, then validate the refactored prompt against the full golden set before deploying it.
The TechDebtRecord Data Structure

Each item in the technical debt register is a TechDebtRecord that captures the debt's type, impact, interest rate (how quickly it is becoming more expensive to carry), and ownership. The interest rate concept — borrowed from financial debt — captures the fact that some technical debt remains cheap to carry indefinitely (a suboptimal variable name) while other debt compounds quickly (a missing circuit breaker that causes cascading failures when the upstream service degrades).

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum

class DebtCategory(str, Enum):
    PROMPT         = "prompt"          # system prompt quality and coherence
    INTEGRATION    = "integration"     # API client code, error handling, retries
    TEST           = "test"            # missing or incomplete evaluation coverage
    MODEL_MGMT     = "model_mgmt"      # version management, upgrade gates
    OPS            = "ops"             # monitoring, alerting, runbook coverage

class DebtImpact(str, Enum):
    HIGH   = "high"    # causing or likely to cause user-facing failures
    MEDIUM = "medium"  # accumulating; will become high-impact if deferred
    LOW    = "low"     # low urgency; register to track

class InterestRate(str, Enum):
    COMPOUNDING = "compounding"  # worsens automatically over time (e.g. stale prompt patches)
    STABLE      = "stable"       # cost stays constant until addressed
    DECLINING   = "declining"    # losing relevance over time (e.g. deprecated API usage)

@dataclass
class TechDebtRecord:
    debt_id: str
    title: str
    category: DebtCategory
    impact: DebtImpact
    interest_rate: InterestRate
    description: str
    date_identified: date
    identified_by: str
    owner: str
    target_resolution_date: date | None = None
    resolution_notes: str = ""
    resolved: bool = False

    def is_overdue(self, today: date) -> bool:
        if self.resolved or not self.target_resolution_date:
            return False
        return today > self.target_resolution_date

    def priority_label(self) -> str:
        if self.impact == DebtImpact.HIGH:
            return "P1" if self.interest_rate == InterestRate.COMPOUNDING else "P2"
        if self.impact == DebtImpact.MEDIUM:
            return "P2" if self.interest_rate == InterestRate.COMPOUNDING else "P3"
        return "P3" if self.interest_rate == InterestRate.COMPOUNDING else "P4"

    def register_line(self) -> str:
        status = "RESOLVED" if self.resolved else ("OVERDUE" if self.is_overdue(date.today()) else "OPEN")
        return f"[{self.priority_label()}] {self.debt_id}: {self.title} ({self.category.value}) — {status}"

The priority_label method combines impact and interest rate to produce a four-tier priority. High-impact compounding debt is P1 because it is both causing problems now and becoming more expensive every week it is not addressed. High-impact stable debt is P2 — it needs to be addressed but is not worsening. Low-impact stable debt is P4, appropriate for backlog tracking with no urgency. The only combination that warrants special attention is medium-impact compounding debt, which can appear manageable ("just medium impact") while actually accelerating toward a P1 incident.
The TechDebtRegister and Reporting

The register aggregates all debt records into a queryable collection and produces the reports that feed the quarterly debt review. The two most important reports are the high-impact open items list (for weekly standups and sprint planning) and the category breakdown (for identifying which type of debt is accumulating fastest, which informs where to invest prevention effort).

The register_health method surfaces three concerns: high-impact open items (which should have near-zero count in a well-managed integration), overdue items (which indicate the team made commitments it did not keep), and dominant category accumulation (which indicates a systematic neglect of one debt type). Prompt debt accumulation specifically gets a named check because it is the most common pattern in Claude integrations and the one most likely to be invisible to teams that are tracking only code-level debt.
Integrating the Debt Register with Sprint Planning

A technical debt register that is not integrated with sprint planning is a documentation artefact, not a management tool. The register must feed into the sprint backlog each cycle, with debt items competing for sprint capacity alongside feature work and bug fixes. The standard allocation for Claude integration teams is 20% of sprint capacity for debt reduction, which ensures that accumulated debt does not grow faster than it is addressed without consuming so much capacity that feature development stalls.
Debt Priority
Sprint Allocation
Review Cadence
Escalation if Not Addressed
P1 (high-impact compounding)
Current sprint — non-negotiable
Daily standup
Escalate to engineering lead same day
P2 (high-impact stable or medium compounding)
Within 2 sprints
Weekly debt review
Flag in weekly review if slipping
P3 (medium-impact stable)
Within quarter
Quarterly debt review
Reclassify to P2 if still open next quarter
P4 (low-impact)
Opportunistic
Quarterly debt review
Close as won't-fix after 2 quarters with no action

The P4 row includes an explicit closure policy: items that sit at P4 for two quarters with no action should be closed as won't-fix rather than remaining in the register indefinitely. A register that grows without ever shrinking loses its signal value — the high-impact items get lost in the noise of stale low-priority items that no one is actually going to address. Explicitly closing stale low-priority items keeps the register signal-to-noise ratio high enough to be useful as a management tool.
Quarterly Debt Review Process

The quarterly debt review is a structured meeting that evaluates the register's health, re-prioritises any items whose context has changed, and identifies systematic debt patterns that require strategic investment rather than item-by-item resolution. It is distinct from sprint-level debt discussions, which focus on individual items; the quarterly review focuses on the aggregate picture and the root causes behind repeated debt accumulation in the same category.

The category_trends field is the most important output of the quarterly review for strategic planning. A category that is "increasing" quarter-over-quarter has a systemic accumulation problem — individual sprint items are not keeping up with the rate of debt creation. The correct response to an increasing trend is not to add more items to the backlog but to identify and address the root cause of accumulation: why is prompt debt increasing? Because the team is not scheduling refactor sprints? Because there is no clear ownership of system prompt quality? Because the product requirements are changing faster than the prompt can be adapted cleanly? The root cause determines the strategic investment.
Preventing Debt Accumulation at the Source

Managing a technical debt register reduces the cost of existing debt but does not prevent new debt from accumulating. Prevention requires integrating debt awareness into the development workflow: making it as easy to register new debt at the point of creation as it is to defer it, and embedding debt prevention into the definition of done for new work. A team that has no mechanism for registering debt at creation will always have a register that understates the true debt burden, because informal debt (changes made without registration) is the most common form.
Debt registration in code review:

When a reviewer identifies a workaround or shortcut in a PR, they add a comment with the debt category and impact. The PR author creates a TechDebtRecord before the PR is merged rather than after. This makes debt registration a first-class part of the code review process.
Prompt change log with patch tracking:

Every change to a system prompt is logged as a PromptPatch record with the reason and model version. This makes prompt debt visible as it accumulates and triggers the refactor threshold automatically when the patch count reaches 10.
Definition of done includes debt assessment:

The team's definition of done for any sprint item includes a debt assessment: did this work create new debt? If yes, register it before marking the item complete. This prevents the pattern where "done" means "feature shipped" but leaves unregistered debt behind.
Debt ceiling by category:

Set a maximum number of open items per debt category that triggers a mandatory sprint allocation. If prompt debt exceeds 8 open items, at least one prompt debt item must be in the next sprint regardless of other priorities. The ceiling prevents any single category from growing unbounded.

The definition of done debt assessment is the most culturally significant change on this list. It reframes "technical debt" from something that accumulates passively — a natural byproduct of moving fast — to something that is consciously chosen and registered. When the team explicitly asks "does this work create new debt?" as part of every sprint item, debt becomes a visible decision rather than an invisible accumulation. Teams that adopt this practice typically find that their register grows quickly at first (as previously invisible debt becomes visible) and then stabilises as the team develops habits that reduce debt creation at the source.
Summary

The technical debt register for Claude teams tracks five debt categories — Prompt, Integration, Test, Model Management, and Ops — across three impact tiers. The PromptDebtRecord tracks system prompt patches individually to flag when refactoring is needed. The TechDebtRecord combines impact and interest rate into a four-tier priority label, with high-impact compounding debt at P1 and low-impact stable debt at P4. The TechDebtRegister aggregates records into category breakdowns and health checks that surface the three most common failure patterns: open high-impact items, overdue commitments, and dominant category accumulation. Sprint integration uses a 20% debt capacity allocation with fixed timelines by priority tier and explicit closure policies for stale P4 items.
